transcript:
[+] This audio recording is intended for use with various automatic speech recognition services to generate machine transcripts. \\||


[+] We will submit the same audio to multiple services to obtain word-level timecodes indicating when each word in the recording begins and ends. \\

[+] By comparing the word-accuracy and timecode-accuracy provided by each service, we can analyze the performance of each service on a per-word basis. \\||


[+] Since the spoken words in the source audio submitted to all A.S.R. services are consistent in wording and timing, we can directly compare how each service formats their word-timecode data. \\

[+] Understanding the structure of each timed-text file format will allow us to develop a converter that translates between them. \\||


[+] Initially, we will compare formats from YouTube's Automatic Captions, Deepgram's Nova, Assembly A.I.'s format, Gentle's format, and a variant of OpenAI's Whisper (WhisperX). \\

[+] We may also consider formats from Otter A.I., HappyScribe, and Descript, although these are paid services and may not be included immediately. \\||


[+] Our goal is to create a superset timed-text format that incorporates all features from the individual formats. \\

[+] This includes translating exclusive features of each format to the syntax of others, even if the original tools for each source format cannot read the extra features by default. \\

[+] For example, Whisper, Deepgram, and Assembly AI provide a confidence score for each word, while Gentle and YouTube do not. \\

[+] Conversely, Gentle offers per-phoneme timing data, whereas others only provide per-word timecodes. \\||


[+] Thank you for your attention. \\

[+] We hope this data will shed light on the features of each format. \\||
